web-tokens.title: Hardcoded drop shadow bypasses --shadow-card-hover token (x2)
web-tokens.detail: Both the tab pill and the floating search button declare `box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 1px var(--white-a08), 0 12px 40px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.45)`. The drop-shadow layer has identical geometry to the token --shadow-card-hover (0 12px 40px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.55)) but a one-off 0.45 alpha, creating a near-duplicate shadow value outside the elevation system. Either reuse the token or promote a --shadow-bar token; as written it will silently drift if the elevation tokens are retuned.
web-tokens.evidence:
box-shadow:
  inset 0 0 0 1px var(--white-a08),
  0 12px 40px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.45);  /* token is rgb(0 0 0 / 0.55) */
web-tokens.verifyReason: Reproduced. TabBar.css lines 27-29 and 64-66 both declare `0 12px 40px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.45)`; tokens.css:131 defines --shadow-card-hover as `0 12px 40px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.55)` — identical geometry, one-off alpha. Production bundle (index-D-lcfRyp.css) contains exactly 2x `12px 40px #00000073` (0x73 = 0.451 alpha) on .tabbar/.tabbar-search vs 1x the token value `12px 40px rgb(0 0 0 / .55)`. Not covered by the intentional list: that exemption is for rgb(13 12 11 / X) page-color scrims and 1-3px hairlines; this is a pure-black drop shadow with non-trivial geometry. Even if the lighter alpha is a deliberate choice for the glass bar, the finding already accommodates that (promote a --shadow-bar token), so it cannot be refuted as intentional. Minor is correctly calibrated for a near-duplicate elevation value that will drift if tokens are retuned.

web-tokens.title: Continue-card play glyph sized off the 4px grid (34px box, 10px padding)
web-tokens.detail: .continue-card-play svg uses width/height 34px and padding 10px — neither is on the 4px spacing grid and neither is a 1-3px hairline detail. Every sibling dimension in the file is grid-conformant (168/296/124 columns, 4px outline-offset). 32px or 36px box with 8px or 12px (--space-2/--space-3) padding would land on-system with no visible change.
web-tokens.verifyReason: Reproduced. Rail.css:162-164 has `width: 34px; height: 34px; padding: 10px` and the prod bundle ships `width:34px;height:34px;padding:10px` verbatim. 34 and 10 are not multiples of 4 and are far above the 1-3px hairline allowance; with the global border-box reset the inner glyph renders 14px — also off-grid. A codebase-wide width scan confirms 34px is the only off-grid fixed control dimension in the frontend (22/24/32/52/76/96/108/124/132/152/168/200/220/296 are all on-grid; 150 is filed separately). One overstatement in the detail: 'every sibling dimension in the file is grid-conformant' overlooks `padding: 3px 5px` on the badge at Rail.css:107, though that is hairline-scale and does not affect the core claim. Unlike the fluid minmax nit, this is a fixed rendered dimension, so minor is the right severity.

web-tokens.title: View-transition motion bypasses --dur-*/--ease-* tokens
web-tokens.detail: ::view-transition-group(hero-art) hardcodes animation-duration: 420ms and animation-timing-function: cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1) — the bezier is a byte-for-byte duplicate of --ease-out, and CSS custom properties DO resolve inside ::view-transition pseudo-elements (they inherit from :root), so var(--ease-out) would work. ::view-transition-old/new(root) likewise hardcodes 240ms. Neither 420 nor 240 exists on the duration ramp (150/200/300); if longer morph durations are intentional, they should be promoted to tokens (e.g. --dur-morph). The reduced-motion override correctly kills these, so this is a consistency issue only.
web-tokens.verifyReason: Reproduced. global.css:120-122 hardcodes `animation-duration: 420ms; animation-timing-function: cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1)` and lines 134-136 hardcode 240ms; prod bundle ships them as `.42s`/`.24s` with `cubic-bezier(.16,1,.3,1)`. The bezier is byte-identical to --ease-out (tokens.css:136), and the technical claim is correct — ::view-transition pseudo-elements are children of the root element and inherit custom properties from :root, so var(--ease-out) would resolve. Neither 420 nor 240 is on the duration ramp (--dur-fast/base/slow = 150/200/300ms, tokens.css:137-139). The mitigating claim also checks out: global.css:150-154 sets `animation: none !important` on all view-transition pseudos under prefers-reduced-motion. Pure consistency issue with a correct fix path; minor is right.

web-tokens.title: Browse grid min column 150px is the only off-grid card dimension
web-tokens.detail: The mid-breakpoint poster grid uses `repeat(auto-fill, minmax(150px, 1fr))`; 150 is not a multiple of 4, while every other card width in the codebase is on-grid (108, 124, 168, 200, 296). It is a fluid-grid minimum (the rendered width is fractional anyway), so impact is nil — 148px or 152px would make the system airtight.
web-tokens.verifyReason: Core claim reproduced with one mislabel. Browse.css:204 does contain `repeat(auto-fill, minmax(150px, 1fr))` (also in the prod bundle as `minmax(150px,1fr)`), and a codebase-wide scan confirms 150 is the only non-multiple-of-4 grid minimum/card width (siblings: 200 at line 114, 152 at line 179, 108 at line 209; rails use 168/296/124/132/96 — all on-grid). Correction to the 'where/detail': line 204 is the .genre-grid (3:2 genre tiles) inside the `@media (max-width: 768px)` block — the MOBILE genre grid, not a 'mid-breakpoint poster grid'; the actual poster grid (.browse-grid) uses 152px desktop / 108px mobile, and the evidence comment 'siblings use 200px/108px' mixes the two grids. The mislabel does not change validity or impact: it is a fluid-grid minimum with fractional rendered widths, so impact is nil and nit is the right severity.

web-tokens:refuted: Footer wordmark set at 18px — not on the type ramp — The value reproduces (Footer.css:16 `font: 600 18px/1 var(--font-display)`, prod ships `font:600 18px/1`), but the finding matches the known-intentional rule: NUX wordmark sizes are explicitly allowed to vary per platform/context — the same rationale that exempts wordmark TEXT instances on hi-fi Figma screens from carrying a style. The web footer (18px, tertiary color) vs NavBar (24px) is exactly that sanctioned per-context variance; the wordmark is a logo treatment exempt from the type ramp by design, not a type-ramp violation. The finding itself concedes this ('the design system explicitly allows NUX wordmark sizes to vary per platform/context'), leaving only optional process advice (add a ramp step), which is not a defect. Per the verification rules, findings matching the intentional list must be refuted.

web-a11y.title: Filter chips use role=tablist/tab without the ARIA tabs keyboard pattern or tabpanels
web-a11y.detail: The chip row is announced as a tablist (`role="tablist" aria-label="Content type"`, each chip `role="tab" aria-selected`), but none of the required tabs behavior exists: no roving tabindex (all five chips stay in the Tab order), no Left/Right arrow-key handling, no `aria-controls`, and no element with `role="tabpanel"`. Screen-reader users hear "tab, 1 of 5, selected", expect arrow-key navigation per the ARIA APG, and get nothing. Content remains reachable (they are real buttons), but the announced role lies about the interaction model. WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (and 2.1.1 Keyboard via the pattern's expected operability). Fix: these are filters, not tabs — drop tablist/tab and use plain toggle buttons with `aria-pressed` (or a labelled radiogroup); alternatively implement the full APG tabs pattern (roving tabindex + arrow keys + tabpanel).
web-a11y.evidence: <div className="browse-chips" role="tablist" aria-label="Content type"> … <button type="button" role="tab" aria-selected={chip === c} … onClick={() => setChip(c)}> — no onKeyDown, no tabIndex management, no aria-controls/tabpanel anywhere in the file
web-a11y.verifyReason: Reproduced at Browse.jsx:58-70: role="tablist" + role="tab" + aria-selected on all five chips, with zero supporting pattern — no onKeyDown, no tabIndex management, no aria-controls, and no role="tabpanel" anywhere in the file or src/. Deployed bundle contains the same 'tablist'. Attempted refutation failed: chips remain operable via Tab+Enter (so not a blocker), but the announced role promises arrow-key navigation and panel association that don't exist — a genuine 4.1.2 role-mismatch on the page's primary filter control. Not covered by any known-intentional item. Major stands.

web-a11y.title: Live search/filter results are never announced (no status message)
web-a11y.detail: Typing in the search field or clicking a chip re-renders the results heading, the `browse-count`, and the empty state with no `aria-live`/`role="status"` region. A screen-reader user typing a query gets no feedback that results changed, how many matched, or that "Nothing here yet" appeared. WCAG 4.1.3 Status Messages (AA). Fix: add `aria-live="polite"` (or role=status) to an element announcing e.g. "12 results" — the existing `<span className="browse-count">` at line 91 is a natural host, or a visually-hidden status node debounced per keystroke.
web-a11y.evidence:
{query ? `Results for “${query}”` : 'All Titles'}{films.length}

… {films.length > 0 ? … :
} — no aria-live anywhere in src/
web-a11y.verifyReason: Reproduced: grep for aria-live / role="status" across src/ and the deployed JS bundle returns nothing. Browse.jsx:88-106 re-renders the heading, browse-count, and "Nothing here yet" empty state silently on every keystroke/chip click. Clear WCAG 4.1.3 (AA) gap on the core search interaction; the suggested host (browse-count span at line 91) exists as described. Major is right.

web-a11y.title: document.title is static across all SPA routes
web-a11y.where: frontend/index.html:6 (+ no title management in src/pages/*)
web-a11y.detail: The title is set once to "NUX — Cinema for Curious Minds" and never updated on route change — /browse, /my-list, and /film/:id all keep the same title, so screen-reader users and browser history/tabs cannot distinguish views. WCAG 2.4.2 Page Titled. Fix: set `document.title` per route (e.g. a small useEffect in each page or a shared <Title> helper): "Browse — NUX", "My List — NUX", `${film.title} — NUX`. Pairs with the route-focus finding below.
web-a11y.evidence: <title>NUX — Cinema for Curious Minds</title>; grep for `document.title` in frontend/src returns nothing
web-a11y.verifyReason: Reproduced: index.html:6 sets "NUX — Cinema for Curious Minds" once; grep for document.title in src/ and in the deployed bundle returns zero hits; curl of the live site shows the same single title. Four routes (/, /browse, /my-list, /film/:id) are indistinguishable in tabs/history/AT. Combined with findings 2 and 4 this is the only navigation signal left, and it's absent — major confirmed.

web-a11y.title: No focus management on route change — focus drops to <body> after poster → detail navigation
web-a11y.detail: ScrollToTop only calls `window.scrollTo(0,0)`. When a PosterCard link is activated, the card unmounts on navigation and focus is lost to <body>; nothing announces the new view and the next Tab press restarts from the document top with no context. WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order (route announcement also supports 4.1.3). Fix: on pathname change move focus to the new page's h1 (`tabIndex={-1}` + `.focus({preventScroll:true})`) or add a visually-hidden route announcer.
web-a11y.evidence: function ScrollToTop() { const { pathname } = useLocation(); useEffect(() => window.scrollTo(0, 0), [pathname]); return null; } — no .focus() call or live region in the app shell
web-a11y.verifyReason: Reproduced at App.jsx:11-15: ScrollToTop runs only window.scrollTo(0,0); no .focus() call, tabIndex={-1}, or live-region announcer exists anywhere in src/. PosterCard (Rail.jsx:23) unmounts on navigation, so focus falls to <body>. With no title change (finding 3) and no status region (finding 2), an SR user activating a poster gets literally no signal that anything happened. Major is right for an SPA whose every interaction is route navigation.

web-a11y.title: Footer copyright uses --text-disabled: 2.69:1 contrast, fails AA
web-a11y.detail: `.footer-copy { color: var(--text-disabled) }` paints real, non-disabled content text ("© 2026 NUX") in paper-700 #5c564c on ink-900 #0d0c0b at 12.5px = 2.69:1, far below the 4.5:1 requirement for small text. The disabled-control exemption does not apply to static text. WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum). One-token fix: use `--text-tertiary` (paper-500 = 5.15:1, passes).
web-a11y.verifyReason: Reproduced exactly: Footer.css:36-38 .footer-copy { color: var(--text-disabled) } → paper-700 #5c564c on ink-900 #0d0c0b; independently recomputed contrast = 2.69:1 at 12.5px (inherits .footer-links 12.5px font). Same rule in deployed CSS (footer-copy{color:var(--text-disabled)}). "© 2026 NUX" is static text rendered via Footer on every route, so this is an unconditional 1.4.3 failure (the disabled exemption indeed doesn't apply); the proposed --text-tertiary fix passes at 5.15:1. Unconditional AA text-contrast failures are conventionally rated serious — major stands.

web-a11y.title: Focus indicator is box-shadow with outline:none — invisible in forced-colors / Windows High Contrast Mode
web-a11y.detail: `:focus-visible { outline: none; box-shadow: var(--focus-ring); }` — forced-colors mode strips box-shadows but honors outlines, so keyboard focus becomes completely invisible there. WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible (forced-colors context). Fix: replace `outline: none` with `outline: 2px solid transparent; outline-offset: 2px;` — invisible normally (box-shadow still shows), but forced-colors repaints the transparent outline. The amber two-layer ring itself (ink-900 inset + amber-500, 7.07:1 vs page) is otherwise excellent. Note Browse.css:48-51 also sets `input:focus { outline: none }` but the global :focus-visible ring plus the amber border still applies to text inputs, so that rule alone is not a failure.
web-a11y.evidence: :focus-visible { outline: none; box-shadow: var(--focus-ring); border-radius: var(--radius-xs); } with --focus-ring: 0 0 0 2px var(--surface-page), 0 0 0 4px var(--border-focus)
web-a11y.verifyReason: Reproduced: global.css:60-64 and the deployed CSS both ship :focus-visible{outline:none;box-shadow:var(--focus-ring);border-radius:var(--radius-xs)}. Forced-colors mode forces box-shadow to none but honors outline-style:none, so keyboard focus is invisible there; the transparent-outline fix is the standard remedy. The caveat about Browse.css:48-51 input:focus is accurate (amber border-color still indicates focus on the input). Scoped to forced-colors users only — minor is right.

web-a11y.title: Tertiary text on 72%-opacity glass chrome can fall below 4.5:1 over bright artwork
web-a11y.detail: Idle nav links (14px) and TabBar labels (10.5px) use --text-tertiary (paper-500) on `rgb(13 12 11 / 0.72)` + blur, fixed over scrolling content. On solid ink-900 the pair is 5.15:1 (pass), but the glass is only 72% opaque: over a bright blurred poster (#cccccc) the effective background is ~#424241 → paper-500 drops to 2.65:1; over #999999 → 3.32:1. The hero's top scrim protects the initial state, but mid-scroll bright art sits directly under both bars. WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum). Fix: use --text-secondary (paper-300: 4.32:1 even over the #cccccc worst case) for idle chrome text, or raise the glass alpha to ~0.85.
web-a11y.verifyReason: Math reproduces exactly: NavBar.css:8 and TabBar.css:24 both use rgb(13 12 11 / 0.72) fixed glass; 0.72×ink-900 composited over #cccccc = #424241, and paper-500 on that = 2.65:1 (over #999999 = 3.32:1); paper-300 = 4.32:1 and paper-100 = 8.77:1 over the same worst case, matching the proposed fix. Idle nav links (NavBar.css:43-44, 14px tertiary) and TabBar labels (10.5px tertiary) are real. Failure is conditional on bright content scrolling under the bars (catalog art is mostly dark), so minor — not major — is the right call. Confirmed as filed.

web-a11y.title: Active desktop nav link is distinguished visually by lightness alone
web-a11y.detail: `.nav-links a.active { color: var(--text-primary) }` — the only visual difference between the current page link (paper-100) and the others (paper-500) is text lightness; no underline, weight, or marker. React Router's NavLink does add `aria-current="page"` automatically, so AT users are covered, and the ~3.3:1 luminance step is perceivable — but low-vision users scanning the bar get no shape cue, and hover uses a similar lightness shift. WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color (visual-only aspect; programmatic state passes). Fix: add a small underline/dot to `.active` — the mobile TabBar already does this right (filled paper-100 pill, 17:1).
web-a11y.evidence: .nav-links a { color: var(--text-tertiary) } / .nav-links a.active { color: var(--text-primary) } — no other declaration changes
web-a11y.verifyReason: Facts reproduce (NavBar.css:54-56: only color changes, paper-500 → paper-100; same in deployed CSS), but there is no normative WCAG failure: NavLink emits aria-current="page" (react-router-dom 7.17.0), and the measured luminance delta between active and idle link text is 3.31:1 — above the 3:1 distinguishability threshold (G183-style), with a hover color shift and the amber focus ring as additional cues. The finding itself concedes 1.4.1's programmatic aspect passes. A purely visual-affordance enhancement (add an underline/dot) with no conformance failure is a nit, not a minor.

web-a11y.title: Touch targets under 44px: chips 36px, search-clear 24px, avatar 32px
web-a11y.detail: All pass WCAG 2.2 AA 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum, 24px) — chip height 36px, clear button exactly 24×24, avatar 32×32 — but all miss the 44px HIG/AAA guidance (2.5.5) on touch viewports: the chips and clear button are core mobile interactions, and the avatar stays visible at ≤768px. The clear button at exactly 24px inside the field is the hardest to hit. Fix: chips → 44px (or min-height 44 with same visual pill via padding), browse-clear → 32-44px hit area (padding, keep 12px glyph), avatar → 44px hit area. For completeness: TabBar items (~51×76) and tabbar-search (52×52) pass 44px; .rail-seeall (~13px tall, Rail.css:20) and footer links (12.5px/1, Footer.css:24) survive 2.5.8 only via the spacing exception — adding vertical padding would be cheap insurance.
web-a11y.evidence: .chip { height: 36px; … } / .browse-clear { width: 24px; height: 24px; } / .nav-avatar { width: 32px; height: 32px; } — nav-avatar is not hidden in the ≤768px media query
web-a11y.verifyReason: All measurements reproduce in source and deployed CSS: .chip{height:36px} (Browse.css:80), .browse-clear{width:24px;height:24px} (Browse.css:62-63), .nav-avatar{width:32px;height:32px} (NavBar.css:84-85), and the ≤768px media query hides only .nav-links and .nav-search, so the 32px avatar does stay visible on touch viewports. The finding correctly concedes everything passes WCAG 2.2 AA 2.5.8 (24px) — the gap is the 44px HIG/AAA bar. Given this project explicitly targets Apple HIG/iOS 26 conventions, minor (not nit) is fair, with the 24px in-field clear button as the sharpest case. Confirmed as filed.

web-a11y.title: No skip link to bypass the fixed header
web-a11y.detail: Keyboard users tab through wordmark + 3 nav links + search + avatar (6 stops) on every page before reaching content. WCAG 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks is technically satisfied via landmarks (header/nav[aria-label]/main/footer are all present and correct — verified on all four pages, exactly one h1 each, no heading-level skips), but landmarks only help AT users; sighted keyboard users get nothing. Fix: a visually-hidden-until-focused "Skip to content" link before <NavBar /> targeting the page <main>.
web-a11y.evidence: App shell renders <NavBar /> before <Routes>; no skip link element exists in the codebase (grep 'skip' returns nothing)
web-a11y.verifyReason: Reproduced: no skip link in App.jsx:17-31 or anywhere in src/ or the deployed bundle (grep 'skip'/'Skip to content' returns nothing). Landmark claims verified — header/nav[aria-label=Primary]/main/footer present, one h1 per page (Hero, Browse, MyList, FilmDetail) — so 2.4.1 has a sufficient technique (ARIA11) and this is not a normative failure; but sighted keyboard users face 6 chrome tab stops, compounded by finding 4 (focus resets to body on every route change, forcing the full re-traverse). Minor is the right level.

web-a11y.title: Cast photo alt duplicates the adjacent visible name
web-a11y.detail:
`` is immediately followed by `

{person.name}

`, so screen readers announce each cast member twice ("Joseph Cotten, image. Joseph Cotten, Holly Martins"). Not a 1.1.1 Non-text Content failure (an alternative exists) but a redundancy defect against the project's otherwise consistent decorative-alt strategy — every other img (hero, posters, stills, genres, editorial, avatar) correctly uses alt="" with adjacent text, all verified. Fix: alt="" on the cast photo, matching the rest.
web-a11y.verifyReason: Reproduced at FilmDetail.jsx:64: alt={person.name} immediately followed by the identical visible name at line 65 — each cast member announced twice. The consistency claim verified by grep: all seven other instances in the codebase (Browse genre, Home editorial, FilmDetail hero, both Rail cards, NavBar avatar, Hero backdrop) use alt="" with adjacent text. Not a 1.1.1 failure, as the finding states, but a real SR-verbosity/consistency defect on every detail page. Minor confirmed.

web-a11y.title: Genre cards are <button> elements with no action
web-a11y.detail: Each genre card is a real `<button type="button">` (good semantics) but has no onClick — it focuses, announces as a button, and does nothing when activated. Same scope-cut family as the stubbed Play/See-all/footer links, so filed as nit only: when genre filtering ships, wire it; until then consider `aria-disabled` or rendering as a non-interactive element so AT users aren't promised an action.
web-a11y.evidence: — no onClick handler
web-a11y.verifyReason: Reproduced at Browse.jsx:78-83: real <button type="button"> with no onClick — focusable, announced as actionable, does nothing. Checked against the known-intentional list: genre cards are NOT among the enumerated scope cuts (See-all, footer anchors, Play, avatar, Games/Courses chips, My List), and the policy for that family permits nit-level flagging, which is exactly how it's filed. Confirmed at nit.

web-a11y.title: Stray declaration: .poster-card outline-offset has no effect
web-a11y.detail: `.poster-card { outline-offset: 4px }` is dead: the global `:focus-visible` rule replaces the outline with a box-shadow ring (outline: none), so outline-offset never renders. Either remove it, or — if the intent was a breathing-room focus ring on posters — it dovetails with the forced-colors fix: switch the global rule to a transparent outline and this offset starts working. Code-hygiene scan otherwise clean: all .map() calls keyed (film ids, item.filmId, person.name, label, chip string, g.id), `viewTransition` is a valid react-router v7 Link prop, and lowercase `fetchpriority` passes through React 18 without console warnings.
web-a11y.evidence: .poster-card { display: grid; gap: var(--space-2); outline-offset: 4px; } vs global.css :focus-visible { outline: none; box-shadow: var(--focus-ring); }
web-a11y.verifyReason: Reproduced: Rail.css:58 ships outline-offset:4px on .poster-card (also in deployed CSS: .poster-card{display:grid;gap:var(--space-2);outline-offset:4px}) while global.css:60-64 sets outline:none in :focus-visible, so the offset never renders — dead code, correctly noted as dovetailing with the forced-colors fix. Side claims verified: react-router-dom 7.17.0 (viewTransition is a valid v7 Link prop), react 18.3.1 (lowercase fetchpriority passes through without warnings), and every .map() in Browse/FilmDetail/TabBar/Rail call sites is keyed. Nit confirmed.

web-a11y.title: Verified pass (no action): tertiary metadata contrast, document basics, reduced motion, keyboard reach
web-a11y.detail: Refuting the brief's suspected failure: --text-tertiary paper-500 #8a8275 on page ink-900 #0d0c0b measures 5.15:1 (not ~4.6:1), so 12.5px metadata, hero meta, fd-details dt, and browse-count all PASS 4.5:1 on solid surfaces (on raised ink-800: 4.84:1, also passes — placeholder included; the only tertiary risk is the translucent-glass case filed separately). Also verified passing: html lang="en", descriptive title, viewport without maximum-scale/user-scalable locks (1.4.4); amber-500 eyebrows 7.07:1; ink-900-on-amber-500 buttons 7.07:1; active chip/TabBar pill 17.03:1; prefers-reduced-motion kills all animations, transitions AND ::view-transition-* pseudos (2.3.3 coverage incl. the poster→hero morph); every action is a real <a>/<button> and keyboard-reachable including both TabBar tabs and the detached search FAB; the two "Primary" nav landmarks are never exposed simultaneously (mutually exclusive display:none at the 768px breakpoint); Browse search input has type="search" + aria-label; search autofocus via ?search=1 is user-initiated.
web-a11y.verifyReason: All spot-checks independently reproduce: contrast(#8a8275,#0d0c0b)=5.15:1, contrast(#8a8275,#161412)=4.84:1 (search input bg is ink-800, placeholder included), contrast(#c8922a,#0d0c0b)=7.07:1, contrast(#f2efe9,#0d0c0b)=17.03:1, ink-900-on-amber 7.07:1. html lang="en" and viewport without scale locks confirmed in index.html and on the live site; prefers-reduced-motion block (global.css:140-156) covers animations, transitions, AND all four ::view-transition-* pseudos; the two aria-label="Primary" navs are mutually exclusive at 768px (NavBar.css:104-107 hides .nav-links; TabBar.css:10/75-79 shows .tabbar-wrap only ≤768); all actions are <a>/<button>/NavLink including TabBar items and the detached search FAB; Browse input has type="search" + aria-label; ?search=1 autofocus is reached only via explicit Search links (NavBar.jsx:21, TabBar.jsx:49). Pass-verification stands.

web-responsive.title: Film detail page overflows horizontally at every viewport below ~1440px — body sections pinned at intrinsic rail width
web-responsive.where: /film/the-third-man + /film/brief-encounter at 1280x800 (144px), 1024x768 (400px), 834x1112 (590px), 768x1024 (204px), 390x844 (582px), 360x780 (612px); root cause frontend/src/pages/FilmDetail.css:74-80 (.fd-body { display: grid })
web-responsive.detail: On every /film/* route, document.scrollingElement.scrollWidth exceeds innerWidth at all tested widths under 1440 (it fits at 1440/1920 by luck: 1424 <= 1425 available). The whole page pans sideways into dead black space; the 'More Like This' rail and 'Cast & Crew' row do NOT scroll internally (rail-scroll scrollWidth == clientWidth), so users reach cards/details values by dragging the page; the rail's 'See all' link sits at x=899-956 on a 390px screen, and details dd values extend to x=736 (e.g. 'Joseph Cotten, Alida Valli, Orson Welles' is ~2 screens to the right). Mechanism: .fd-body is display:grid, so section.rail/.fd-section are grid items whose min-width:auto resolves to the rail's intrinsic min-content width — desktop: 7 posters x 168px + 6 x 20px gap + 2 x 64px padding = exactly 1424px; <=768 media query: 7 x 124px + 6 x 12px + 2 x 16px + scroll padding = 972px. Home is unaffected because rails there sit in a block container. Live-verified fix on production DOM: adding `.fd-body > * { min-width: 0; max-width: 100%; }` collapses overflow to 0 and makes both .rail-scroll and .fd-cast proper internal scrollers (also works: making .fd-body display:block; NOT sufficient: grid-template-columns: minmax(0,1fr) alone, or min-width:0 alone — Chromium still sizes the track to the scroll container's intrinsic width without the max-width clamp).
web-responsive.evidence: Sweep offenders: section.fd-section/section.rail right=1424,left=0 at 1280/1024/834; right=972 at 768/390/360. Screenshot audit-834-film.jpeg shows nav+hero at 834px with body sections extending to 1424px; audit-390-film.jpeg shows sections laid out across 972px vs 390px viewport. Fix test: overflow 582 -> -15, railScrollable false -> true, railW 972 -> 375.
web-responsive.verifyReason: Reproduced from code + deployed bundle (index-D-lcfRyp.css identical to source). FilmDetail.css:74-80 has .fd-body{display:grid}; grid items .rail/.fd-section keep overflow:visible so min-width:auto = min-content contribution, which includes the inner scroll container's full content (the scroll container .rail-scroll is not the grid item, so the zero-automatic-minimum rule does not apply). Token math matches the claim exactly: desktop 7x168 + 6x20 (--space-5:20px) + 2x64 (--space-16:64px) = 1424px; <=768: 7x124 + 6x12 (--space-3) + 2x16 (--space-4) = 972px (rail-scroll's 8px padding nets out against its -8px margin). All six reported overflow deltas equal track-minus-viewport precisely (144/400/590/204/582/612), and related for the-third-man yields exactly 7 PosterCards (18 FILM candidates, slice(0,7)). No min-width:0 or max-width clamp exists anywhere in the production CSS. Home.css confirms .home-rails is a plain block, so only /film/* is hit — consistent. Core content page unusable below 1440 (page pans into dead space, rails not internally scrollable): blocker is the right severity.

web-responsive.title: Footer links permanently hidden behind floating TabBar at <=768 — no bottom padding compensation on any page
web-responsive.where: All routes (/, /browse, /my-list, /film/*, /browse?search=1) at 768x1024, 390x844, 360x780; frontend/src/components/TabBar.css:4-16 (fixed, bottom:12px, z-index:100) — no matching padding-bottom anywhere in src/**.css
web-responsive.detail: The iOS-26 floating tab bar is position:fixed at the bottom with z-index 100, but no page, footer, or body rule adds bottom padding at <=768 (grep over all CSS finds zero padding-bottom compensation). At maximum scroll the footer's link row (About / Help / Privacy / Terms / (c) 2026 NUX) sits inside the tab bar's box and can never be scrolled clear of it: at 390x844 the links' bottom edge is y=820 while the tab bar occupies y=773-832; at 768x1024 links bottom=1000 vs bar 953-1012; at 360x780 links bottom=750-756 vs bar 709-768. The pill and the detached search circle (pointer-events:auto) visually cover and intercept taps on these links — they are illegible and mostly untappable on phones. /my-list at 768 escapes only because the page is shorter than the viewport. Fix: add ~96px bottom padding to the footer (or main) inside the <=768 media query, e.g. padding-bottom: calc(76px + env(safe-area-inset-bottom) + var(--space-6)).
web-responsive.evidence: Screenshot audit-390-footer-tabbar.jpeg (scrolled to scrollTop=4085, the max) shows 'About' half-behind the pill's left edge and '(c) 2026 NUX' behind the search circle. Sweep behindTabbar arrays list all 5 footer links + wordmark overlapping the tab bar box on every scrollable route at 768/390/360.
web-responsive.verifyReason: Reproduced from code: TabBar.css:4-16 is position:fixed, bottom:calc(12px + safe-area), z-index:100, display:flex at <=768, with pointer-events:auto on .tabbar and .tabbar-search. grep over all source CSS AND the deployed bundle finds zero padding-bottom declarations — no inset compensation exists. Geometry checks out: footer-inner's only bottom inset is 24px (--space-6), so at max scroll the links row bottom sits 24px above the viewport bottom (844-24=820, matching the reported y=820), inside the pill's computed zone (pill height ~59.5px -> y 772.5-832, matching reported 773-832). Both bars are horizontally centered (~308px pill+search vs ~327px links row) so they overlap. App.jsx renders Footer+TabBar on every route. The links being #anchor stubs is on the intentional list, but this finding is about occlusion/missing content inset, which is not — and it breaks the iOS floating-tab-bar pattern the project explicitly emulates, on every scrollable route on phones. Major stands.

web-responsive.title: Footer floats mid-screen on short pages — ~240px of bare page background below it (no sticky-footer layout)
web-responsive.where: /my-list at 1440x900 and 834x1112 (also 1920x1080); frontend/src/styles/global.css:17-24 (body has min-height:100vh but #root has no flex column layout)
web-responsive.detail: On routes whose content is shorter than the viewport (My List with 6 items), the footer renders directly after the grid and ~240px (1440x900) / ~150px (834) of empty ink-900 background hangs below it, making the page look unfinished. Standard fix: #root { min-height: 100vh|100dvh; display: flex; flex-direction: column; } with main { flex: 1; } so the footer anchors to the viewport bottom on short pages.
web-responsive.evidence: Screenshot audit-1440-mylist.jpeg: footer bottom at ~y=660 of a 900px viewport with dead space below; audit-834-mylist.jpeg shows the same pattern.
web-responsive.verifyReason: Reproduced from code: global.css:17-24 gives body min-height:100vh but no CSS file in src (or the deployed bundle) contains any #root rule or flex-column page shell; main has no flex:1. MyList.jsx renders exactly 6 PosterCards (SAVED array), one grid row at 1440 -> page shorter than 900px viewport, so the footer sits directly after the grid with bare ink-900 below. The exact ~240px figure comes from their screenshot, but the mechanism is unambiguous from code and the proposed fix is standard. Minor (cosmetic, short routes only) is the right severity.

web-responsive.title: Backdrop-less film fallback blows a 2:3 poster up into the 78vh wide-screen hero — giant cropped credits text on desktop/tablet
web-responsive.where: /film/brief-encounter at 1440x900 and 834x1112; frontend/src/pages/FilmDetail.jsx:26 (src={film.backdrop || film.poster}) + FilmDetail.css:14-18 (object-fit: cover)
web-responsive.detail: Brief Encounter (no backdrop, no synopsis, no cast) falls back to the portrait poster as hero art. object-fit:cover crops a 2:3 image into a ~16:6 region, so at 1440 the hero is dominated by a heavily magnified slice of the poster's credits block ('CELIA JOHNSON / TREVOR HOWARD / STANLEY HOLLOWAY...') — low-res, awkward focal crop, and visually competes with the real H1 underneath. At 390 (portrait viewport) the same fallback composes acceptably. Everything else about the fallback is correct: cast section properly omitted, details table shows only Genre/Release, no 'undefined' text, no console errors. Suggest a poster-specific hero treatment (blurred poster fill + scrim, or shorter hero) when film.backdrop is absent.
web-responsive.evidence: Screenshot audit-1440-film-fallback.jpeg vs audit-390-film-fallback.jpeg; live DOM: .fd-art img naturalWidth/Height ratio 2:3 stretched over a 1440x~630 box.
web-responsive.verifyReason: Reproduced from code: catalog.js:34 shows brief-encounter has poster only (no backdrop, synopsis, or cast); FilmDetail.jsx:26 uses src={film.backdrop || film.poster}; .fd-hero{min-height:78vh} + .fd-art img{object-fit:cover; width/height:100%} (FilmDetail.css:1-18) means a 2:3 portrait image is width-fit to 1440px (~2160px tall) and center-cropped to ~700px — a ~3.4x magnified low-res middle slice. The claim that the visible slice is specifically the credits block rests on their screenshot, but the crop-magnification defect itself is unambiguous from code, and the secondary claims check out (cast section conditionally omitted via {film.cast && ...}, details reduce to Genre/Release since only genre and year exist, details.filter removes undefined values). At 390x844 the hero box (~0.59 aspect) nearly matches 2:3, so phone composes acceptably — consistent. Minor confirmed: one film, fallback path, content still readable.

web-responsive.title: Details table keeps the fixed 160px dt column at phone widths — values squeezed to ~174px once the overflow bug is fixed
web-responsive.where: /film/the-third-man at 390x844 and 360x780; frontend/src/pages/FilmDetail.css:94-100 (.fd-details-row { grid-template-columns: 160px 1fr; gap: var(--space-6) })
web-responsive.detail: There is no <=768 override for .fd-details-row, so the label column stays 160px + 24px gap. Today this is masked by the page-level overflow (dd values render at x=200-736 on a 972px-wide section); once finding #1 is fixed the dd column gets 390 - 32 (padding) - 160 - 24 = 174px (144px at 360) — long values like the 3-name Cast row wrap to 3+ lines against a half-empty label column. Recommend narrowing dt (e.g. 96px) and gap to var(--space-4) under 768px, or stacking dt above dd.
web-responsive.evidence: Live measurement at 390: sectionW=972, dtW=160, ddW=536, ddRight=736 (pre-fix); simulated post-fix dd width 174px. dt strings are short ('Director', 'Runtime') — max ~70px of actual text.
web-responsive.verifyReason: Reproduced from code: FilmDetail.css:94-100 hard-codes grid-template-columns:160px 1fr with gap --space-6 (24px), and the only <=768 media query (lines 145-156) adjusts just .fd-content/.fd-section padding and .fd-title — no .fd-details-row override, in source or the deployed bundle. Post-fix arithmetic verified with token values: 390 - 2x16 (--space-4 section padding) - 160 - 24 = 174px; 360 - 32 - 160 - 24 = 144px. The Cast dd is the 3-name slice(0,3) join ('Joseph Cotten, Alida Valli, Orson Welles', confirmed in catalog.js), ~250px+ at 14px, so 2-3+ wrapped lines against a half-empty 160px label column whose longest label ('Language'/'Director') needs ~70px. Correctly framed as latent (currently masked by finding 1). Minor is right.

web-perf.title: 510KB PNG for a 32px avatar, loaded on every page
web-perf.detail: avatar-user.png is 510,157 B at 600x600 px, but .nav-avatar renders it at 32x32 (64px needed @2x DPR) — ~9.4x linear / ~88x pixel-count oversize. NavBar mounts on every route, so this is 510KB of the cold-load on /, /browse, /my-list and /film/:id alike. A 64-96px JPEG/WebP crop would be ~3-8KB, a >99% saving and the single cheapest win in the whole audit.
web-perf.evidence: curl -sI: content-length: 510157; sips: 600x600; NavBar.css .nav-avatar { width: 32px; height: 32px }
web-perf.verifyReason: Fully reproduced. curl -sI returns content-length: 510157 with content-type: image/png; sips confirms 600x600. NavBar.css:83-89 renders .nav-avatar at 32x32px (img at 100%/object-fit:cover), i.e. 64px needed at 2x DPR — 9.4x linear / ~88x pixel-count oversize. App.jsx mounts <NavBar /> outside <Routes>, so the image is part of cold load on any entry route (/, /browse, /my-list, /film/:id). Largest single wasted asset relative to rendered size in the app; major is the right severity.

web-perf.title: LCP hero image is 548KB and discovered only after JS executes
web-perf.detail: The Home hero backdrop (still-lawrence-of-arabia-2.jpg) is 548,448 B at 1600x669 — over the 400KB budget; a q~70 re-encode of the same 1600w frame should land ~250-300KB. Dimensions are fine for a full-width hero. Bigger issue: the URL lives in the JS bundle, and index.html has no <link rel=preload>, so the LCP request chain is HTML → 74.8KB JS (gzip) → React render → image fetch. fetchpriority="high" is correctly set (verified in Hero.jsx, and React 18 passes the lowercase attribute through to the DOM) but only helps after late discovery. Add <link rel="preload" as="image" href="/assets/stills/still-lawrence-of-arabia-2.jpg" fetchpriority="high"> to index.html plus recompress.
web-perf.evidence: content-length: 548448; sips 1600x669; dist/index.html head contains only the JS/CSS tags — no preload
web-perf.verifyReason: Reproduced. content-length: 548448, sips 1600x669. The served index.html head contains exactly charset, viewport, title, the module script (/assets/index-NILHAvcl.js) and stylesheet — no <link rel=preload>, so the request chain is HTML → 74,837 B gzip JS → React render → image. Hero.jsx:11 does set fetchpriority="high" (lowercase; React 18.3.1 per package.json passes unknown lowercase attrs to the DOM, so the claim about React 18 is accurate), which only helps after late discovery. Recompress + preload recommendation is sound. Major holds for the page's LCP asset.

web-perf.title: Continue Watching stills ship 1600px wide for a ≤330px rail; two exceed 400KB
web-perf.detail: The 4 Continue Watching stills on / total 1,188,107 B near the top of the page: still-lawrence-of-arabia.jpg 411,131 B (1600x670) and still2-red-shoes.jpg 447,662 B (1600x1298) both break the 400KB cap; still-third-man.jpg 162,396 B and still-black-narcissus.jpg 166,918 B are 1600px wide too. ContinueCards render ≤330px wide (660px @2x), so sources carry ~2.4x linear / ~5.9x pixel-count excess. Resizing to 660w would cut this group ~75-80% (~1.19MB → ~0.25-0.30MB). Note still2-red-shoes doubles as the Red Shoes film-detail backdrop, so either keep a large variant for that route or serve two sizes.
web-perf.evidence: sips + stat: 411,131 B @1600x670; 447,662 B @1600x1298; 4-file sum 1,188,107 B; rendered ≤330px per rail spec
web-perf.verifyReason: All numbers exact: still-lawrence-of-arabia.jpg 411,131 B @1600x670, still2-red-shoes.jpg 447,662 B @1600x1298, still-third-man.jpg 162,396 B @1600x1196, still-black-narcissus.jpg 166,918 B @1600x1103; sum 1,188,107 B. Home.jsx renders the Continue Watching rail with wide=true → Rail.css .rail-scroll--wide grid-auto-columns: 296px, so the ≤330px render claim is correct (actually 296px, making the oversize slightly worse than stated). catalog.js confirms still2-red-shoes.jpg doubles as the-red-shoes' film-detail backdrop, so the two-size caveat is accurate. 1.19MB of above-the-fold-adjacent imagery justifies major.

web-perf.title: Poster set is ~2.5x oversize: 3.15MB across 21 JPEGs for ≤220px cards
web-perf.where: frontend/public/assets/posters/ (used by all 3 Home rails and the full /browse grid)
web-perf.detail: All 21 referenced posters are ~700x1050 px (86,969-235,044 B each, sum 3,146,274 B) but render at ≤220px wide (440px needed @2x) — ~1.6x linear / ~2.5x pixel-count more than needed even for retina. Heaviest: poster-lawrence-of-arabia.jpg 235,044 B, poster-third-man.jpg 215,714 B, poster-peeping-tom.jpg 206,099 B. Resizing to 440x660 would roughly halve the set to ~1.4-1.6MB, benefiting both / (21 posters across rails) and /browse (same 21 in the grid). All are loading="lazy" (verified Rail.jsx:25), which softens but does not remove the cost — Chrome's lazy threshold (1250-2500px) fetches most of them shortly after load anyway.
web-perf.evidence: stat sum 3,146,274 B over 21 files; sips ~700x1050 each vs 220px (440 @2x) rendered
web-perf.verifyReason: Reproduced exactly: 21 poster files sum 3,146,274 B; sips shows 684-756 x 1050 across the set; heaviest are poster-lawrence-of-arabia.jpg 235,044 B, poster-third-man.jpg 215,714 B, poster-peeping-tom.jpg 206,099 B. Render widths verified: Rail.css grid-auto-columns: 168px (desktop rails, 124px mobile) and Browse.css .browse-grid minmax(152px,1fr) (~170px at 1440w) — both under the claimed 220px, so the oversize ratio is actually slightly understated (~2.1x linear at 2x vs the stated 1.6x). loading="lazy" confirmed on the PosterCard img in Rail.jsx. Minor is right: lazy-loaded, individually reasonable files, aggregate cost only.

web-perf.title: Page-weight estimate: / ≈ 6.0MB fully loaded (~2.5MB first viewport), /browse ≈ 5.25MB images
web-perf.detail: Cold / first-viewport: 417 B HTML + 74,837 B JS (gzip; 199,169 raw) + 6,183 B CSS (gzip; 25,145 raw) + 142,960 B fonts (3 latin woff2) + 65,880 B noise-grain.png + 510,157 B avatar + 548,448 B hero + 1,188,107 B CW stills = 2,536,989 B ≈ 2.54MB; with ~6 in-threshold Trending posters (~1.09MB) realistic initial load is ~3.6MB. Fully scrolled / adds the remaining posters (21 total: 3,146,274 B) and the editorial still still-matter-life-death.jpg (329,875 B, lazy, 1600x1278) → ≈ 6.01MB. /browse: 12 genre cards 2,099,761 B (1200x805 each — acceptable for ≤480px cards @2x at 1.25x) + 21 posters 3,146,274 B = 5,246,035 B images + ~224KB shell. ~93% of page weight is imagery; the avatar/hero/CW-still fixes above plus poster resizing would bring / under ~3MB. Code payload itself is healthy for a demo (75KB JS gzip, single bundle, no source maps leaked — .map URLs 404 and the bundle has no sourceMappingURL).
web-perf.evidence: curl size_download 74,837 (JS) / 6,183 (CSS); component sums measured via stat/sips listed per finding
web-perf.verifyReason: Every measurable component re-derived and matches: HTML 417 B, JS 74,837 B gzip (199,169 raw), CSS 6,183 B gzip (25,145 raw), latin font trio 142,960 B, noise-grain.png 65,880 B, avatar 510,157 B, hero 548,448 B, CW stills 1,188,107 B → 2,536,989 B exactly. Full /: + posters 3,146,274 B + still-matter-life-death.jpg 329,875 B (1600x1278, lazy, confirmed as EDITORIAL_PICK.image) = 6,013,138 B ≈ 6.01MB. /browse: 12 GENRES images sum exactly 2,099,761 B (1200x805 each) + 21 posters = 5,246,035 B. Source maps verified absent (.map → 404, no sourceMappingURL in bundle). One quibble that does not change totals: genre cards render ~205px via minmax(200px,1fr) at 1440w, not the assumed ≤480px, so genre imagery is less 'acceptable' than the finding allows — if anything the estimate is conservative. Minor is appropriate for an aggregate estimate.

web-perf.title: index.html served with no Cache-Control — stale-HTML / white-screen window after deploys
web-perf.detail: The / response has etag + last-modified but zero Cache-Control header, so browsers apply heuristic freshness (~10% of time since last-modified). After a redeploy the old cached index.html can keep referencing the previous hashed bundle (e.g. /assets/index-NILHAvcl.js), which no longer exists in the new dist → 404 → blank app until revalidation. Add 'Cache-Control: no-cache' (or max-age=0, must-revalidate) for index.html / the SPA fallback location.
web-perf.evidence: curl -sI https://nux.ontwrpn.com/ | grep -ci cache-control → 0; assets are content-hashed so old filenames disappear on each build
web-perf.verifyReason: Reproduced: curl -sI https://nux.ontwrpn.com/ returns etag "6a2b9669-1a1" and last-modified but zero cache-control header (grep -ci → 0), while assets are content-hashed (index-NILHAvcl.js / index-D-lcfRyp.css) and old filenames disappear on rebuild. Heuristic freshness (~10% of time-since-last-modified) creates a real, growing window where a cached index.html references a vanished bundle → 404 → blank SPA until revalidation. The proposed Cache-Control: no-cache on the SPA fallback is the standard fix. Minor is right: intermittent, self-healing, deploy-frequency-dependent.

web-perf.title: HTML head: favicon request answered with index.html (200 text/html), no meta description, no OG tags
web-perf.detail: There is no <link rel="icon">, and /favicon.ico hits the SPA fallback: HTTP 200, content-type: text/html, content-length: 417 (it is literally index.html). Refines the known 'favicon 404s' — it never 404s, it 200s with HTML, so the browser retries instead of caching a miss and the tab icon stays blank. Head also lacks <meta name="description"> and all og:/twitter: tags (only <title> exists), so shared links render bare — notable for a content platform demo. Fix: add a real favicon (even a 1KB SVG/PNG + link tag) and a description + og:title/og:description/og:image (a poster or hero still already on the domain).
web-perf.evidence: curl -sI /favicon.ico → 200, content-type: text/html, content-length: 417; dist/index.html head = charset, viewport, title, script, stylesheet only
web-perf.verifyReason: Reproduced: curl -sI /favicon.ico → HTTP 200, content-type: text/html, content-length: 417, and the body is literally index.html (SPA fallback), so the browser never caches a miss — the 200-with-HTML refinement is accurate. The served head contains only charset, viewport, <title>, script and stylesheet: no <link rel=icon>, no <meta name=description>, no og:/twitter: tags. Not on the known-intentional list (which covers in-app anchor stubs, not document metadata). Minor fits for a publicly-deployed content-platform demo whose links will be shared.

web-perf.title: Three variable font families shipped (~397KB in dist, ~143KB fetched/page); Newsreader serves a single CSS rule
web-perf.detail: Fontsource imports Fraunces + Inter + Newsreader variable fonts: 13 woff2 files, 406,548 B in dist. Thanks to unicode-range, a Latin page fetches 3 files = 142,960 B (Inter 48,256 + Fraunces 36,620 + Newsreader 58,084) — reasonable. But --font-essay (Newsreader) is used by exactly 1 selector vs 17 for --font-ui and 7 for --font-display; dropping it (or deferring to the editorial route) saves 58KB per cold load and 106KB in dist. Demo-scale judgement call — the tri-font system is a deliberate editorial identity.
web-perf.evidence: grep var(--font-essay) → 1 use; dist woff2 sum 406,548 B; latin trio 142,960 B
web-perf.verifyReason: Byte measurements all reproduce exactly: 13 woff2 in dist totalling 406,548 B; latin trio fetched per page = 142,960 B (Inter 48,256 + Fraunces 36,620 + Newsreader 58,084); dropping Newsreader saves 106,260 B in dist. One factual correction: the 'exactly 1 selector / single CSS rule' claim undercounts because grepping var(--font-essay) only hits the --type-essay token definition in tokens.css:128 — the token is consumed by 2 rules on 2 routes (.editorial-dek in Home.css:25 and .fd-synopsis in FilmDetail.css:63), so removing Newsreader changes visible body text on both Home and FilmDetail, not one obscure rule. The substance (58KB/cold-load for marginal use, deliberate tri-font identity, judgement call) and nit severity stand.

web-perf.title: Hashed JS/CSS/font assets cached only 7 days instead of immutable 1 year
web-perf.detail: Content-hashed bundles (index-NILHAvcl.js, index-D-lcfRyp.css, *-wght-normal-*.woff2) get 'cache-control: public, max-age=604800'. Since filenames change on every content change, these can safely be 'public, max-age=31536000, immutable', eliminating weekly revalidation. Keep 7d for the un-hashed /assets/posters|stills|genres|cast images — that value is appropriate there.
web-perf.evidence: curl -sI on JS/CSS/woff2 → cache-control: public, max-age=604800 on all
web-perf.verifyReason: Reproduced on all three asset classes: curl -sI on /assets/index-NILHAvcl.js, /assets/index-D-lcfRyp.css and /assets/inter-latin-wght-normal-Dx4kXJAl.woff2 each return cache-control: public, max-age=604800. Since Vite content-hashes these filenames, public, max-age=31536000, immutable is strictly safe, and the finding correctly carves out the un-hashed poster/still/genre images where 7d is appropriate. Pure optimization with no user-visible defect — nit is right.

web-perf.title: No brotli — gzip only
web-perf.detail: Requesting with 'Accept-Encoding: br' returns no content-encoding (uncompressed); only gzip is served. Brotli would take the 74,837 B JS transfer to roughly 62-65KB and CSS from 6,183 B to ~5KB. Savings ~12KB/cold load — not worth a custom nginx build for a demo, but free if the base image gains the module.
web-perf.evidence: curl -sI -H 'Accept-Encoding: br' .../index-NILHAvcl.js → no content-encoding header; with gzip,br → content-encoding: gzip
web-perf.verifyReason: Reproduced: requesting index-NILHAvcl.js with Accept-Encoding: br alone returns no content-encoding header (identity), and with gzip, br returns content-encoding: gzip; server is nginx/1.31.1. Savings estimate (~10-13KB on the 74,837 B gzip JS) is in the standard br-vs-gzip range. The finding itself concedes it is not worth a custom nginx build for a demo — nit is the correct severity.

web-perf.title: ~2.2MB of never-referenced images ship in every deploy
web-perf.detail: 7 stills are referenced nowhere in src (grep 0 hits): still-get-carter.jpg, still-get-carter-2.jpg, still2-fallen-idol.jpg (441,453 B), still2-richard-iii.jpg, still2-third-man.jpg (402,838 B), still2-third-man-2.jpg, still2-zulu.jpg — 1,619,662 B total. Plus the EXTRAS images (genre-poster-neondrift.jpg 283,566 B, genre-poster-course.jpg 190,068 B, genre-welcome-bg.jpg 116,072 B ≈ 590KB) — EXTRAS is exported from catalog.js but imported by no page/component. None of this hits users' networks, it only bloats the image dir/Docker image by ~2.2MB; harmless at demo scale, prune when convenient.
web-perf.evidence: grep -rn for those filenames and EXTRAS across src/*.jsx → no consumers; stat sum 1,619,662 B (stills) + 589,706 B (extras)
web-perf.verifyReason: Reproduced exactly. Grep across src/ finds zero references for all 7 stills (still-get-carter.jpg 176,679 + still-get-carter-2.jpg 147,264 + still2-fallen-idol.jpg 441,453 + still2-richard-iii.jpg 170,390 + still2-third-man.jpg 402,838 + still2-third-man-2.jpg 167,583 + still2-zulu.jpg 113,455 = 1,619,662 B, matching the finding to the byte). EXTRAS is exported from catalog.js:129-145 but imported by no page/component (grep EXTRAS outside catalog.js → 0 hits); its three images sum 283,566 + 190,068 + 116,072 = 589,706 B. Correctly scoped as deploy/image-dir bloat only — these never hit user networks since the components referencing them are never rendered. Nit is right.

web-perf:refuted: Fixed full-viewport grain overlay with mix-blend-mode adds a per-frame blend pass on scroll — Matches the known-intentional list: 'Web grain overlay = fixed .grain::after with noise PNG at 2.5% overlay blend — intentional.' The CSS facts do reproduce (global.css:72-83 has position:fixed; inset:0; z-index:9999; background 256px noise tile; opacity:0.025; mix-blend-mode:overlay; the PNG is 65,880 B), but the finding flags exactly the declared-intentional implementation and offers no measured regression — it is purely hypothetical ('fine on desktop GPUs', 'if jank is ever observed'), with no demonstrated jank on any target device. Per the verification rules, findings matching the intentional list must be refuted; keep the opacity-only fallback note as background knowledge, not a finding.

web-ux.title: "More Like This" genre logic is a no-op for films — every film page shows the same first-7 catalog entries
web-ux.detail: related = FILMS.filter(f => f.id !== film.id && (f.genre === film.genre || f.type === film.type)).slice(0, 7). Since 19 of 21 titles share type 'FILM', the type fallback matches EVERY other film, so the genre test is irrelevant: every FILM detail page shows the identical list — The Third Man, Brief Encounter, Black Narcissus, The Red Shoes, Peeping Tom, Lawrence of Arabia, Billy Liar (minus self) — in catalog-definition order. Saint Maud (Horror) recommends a 1945 romance and a 1963 comedy and never reaches the other horrors defined later in the file. DOCs are fine (Senna → Touching the Void only, sparse but relevant). Fix: filter by genre first, then top up with same-type titles only if fewer than 7.
web-ux.evidence: const related = FILMS.filter((f) => f.id !== film.id && (f.genre === film.genre || f.type === film.type)).slice(0, 7);
web-ux.verifyReason: Reproduced by executing the real filter against catalog.js: saint-maud, aftersun and 45-years all return the identical list [the-third-man, brief-encounter, black-narcissus, the-red-shoes, peeping-tom, lawrence-of-arabia, billy-liar]; senna returns only [touching-the-void]. 19 of 21 titles are type FILM, so `f.type === film.type` matches every other film and the genre clause never influences the slice(0,7). Deployed bundle contains the same code (`genre===t.genre||i.type===t.type` … `slice(0,7)`). Saint Maud (Horror) indeed recommends Brief Encounter (Romance 1945) and Billy Liar (Comedy 1963) while Don't Look Now / The Wicker Man (Horror, catalog positions 9-10) never appear. Implemented relevance logic failing on every one of the 19 FILM detail pages justifies major.

web-ux.title: Unknown /film/:id silently falls back to The Third Man under the wrong URL
web-ux.detail: const film = byId(id) || byId('the-third-man'). A deep-link typo (e.g. /film/the-wickerman) renders a full Third Man page while the address bar keeps the bogus id — the user gets the wrong film with no signal anything failed, and can share/bookmark the wrong URL. Should redirect to a 404/Browse state (or at least <Navigate to="/film/the-third-man" replace>) instead of silently lying about identity.
web-ux.verifyReason: Reproduced: FilmDetail.jsx:8 `const film = byId(id) || byId('the-third-man')` and the deployed bundle contains `||vr("the-third-man")`; live /film/the-wickerman returns 200 with the SPA shell, so the client renders The Third Man under the bogus URL. However, severity is overstated: every in-app link (PosterCard, Hero, ContinueCard) is generated from catalog ids, so no navigation path inside the app can produce an unknown id — it requires a hand-edited URL. The sibling finding for the identical failure family (catch-all `*` route rendering Home under any unknown URL) was rated minor; rendering a specific wrong film instead of Home is marginally worse but the same exposure class on a demo with no sharing features. Downgrade major → minor.

web-ux.title: 17 of 21 film pages use a ~250px portrait poster as a full-bleed 78vh backdrop and render nearly empty
web-ux.detail: Only the-third-man, black-narcissus, the-red-shoes and lawrence-of-arabia have a backdrop; everything else falls back to film.poster inside .fd-art (object-fit: cover, min-height 78vh full width — FilmDetail.css:1-18). Per MANIFEST.json the posters are tiny sources (Brief Encounter 228×350, Black Narcissus 260×389, Billy Liar 250×363…), so the hero is a heavily upscaled, heavily cropped blur on most titles. Those same 19 films also lack synopsis/runtime/rating/cast/director, so the page degrades to: eyebrow, title, bare year, two inert buttons, the broken More-Like-This rail, and a 2-row Details table (Genre/Release). Structurally it doesn't break, but the experience gap between the two 'complete' films and the rest is severe — either enrich the data or design an explicit reduced layout (poster-left card instead of fake backdrop hero).
web-ux.evidence: ; MANIFEST.json: poster-brief-encounter.jpg w:228 h:350
web-ux.verifyReason: Counts verified: only the-third-man, black-narcissus, the-red-shoes, lawrence-of-arabia have `backdrop` (4 of 21 → 17 fall back to `film.poster` via FilmDetail.jsx:26), and FilmDetail.css sets .fd-hero min-height 78vh with object-fit: cover. Two factual corrections that don't change the verdict: (1) shipped posters are pre-upscaled to 1050px height — deployed poster-brief-encounter.jpg measures 684×1050, exactly 3x the 228×350 MANIFEST source — so the served file isn't 228px, but its pixel data is still a 3x interpolation of a tiny source, then cover-cropped into a full-width 78vh frame (~6x effective upscale at desktop widths, with ~60% of the portrait cropped away); (2) 'those same 19' slightly conflates sets — 19 lack synopsis/runtime/rating, 20 lack cast/director, while backdrop-less is 17 (black-narcissus and red-shoes have backdrops but no metadata). The substance holds: 17 of 21 detail pages degrade to eyebrow + title + bare year + two inert buttons + identical More-Like-This rail + 2-row details table under a blurry cropped-poster hero. Not on the intentional list; for the product's core page type this is a major experience gap.

web-ux.title: Browse loses chip and search state after visiting a film and going back
web-ux.detail: chip and query live in useState only; nothing is written to the URL (?search=1 is read but the live query/chip never sync to searchParams). Open Browse → select Documentaries → search '19' → tap a result → back: Browse remounts at All with an empty query and the user's filter context is gone. Mirror chip/query into search params (useSearchParams set) so back/forward and sharing preserve state. Side nit: because ?search=1 persists in the URL, returning back to /browse?search=1 re-fires the autofocus effect (Browse.jsx:15-17) and pops the keyboard on mobile.
web-ux.evidence: const [chip, setChip] = useState('All'); const [query, setQuery] = useState('');
web-ux.verifyReason: Reproduced in Browse.jsx:11-12: chip and query live only in useState; useSearchParams is read-only (only `params.has('search')` at line 16) and nothing ever writes state back to the URL. Navigating Browse → /film/:id unmounts Browse, so back remounts it at chip='All', query=''. The side nit is also real: NavBar.jsx:21 and TabBar.jsx:49 both link to /browse?search=1, so returning via back to that URL re-runs the [params] effect and refocuses the input on every remount. Minor is the right severity for in-session filter-state loss in a demo.

web-ux.title: ScrollToTop fires on back/forward too — scroll position never restored
web-ux.detail: useEffect(() => window.scrollTo(0,0), [pathname]) runs on every pathname change including POP navigations, overriding the browser's native scroll restoration (and can produce a restore-then-jump flash). Back from a film to a 4-rail Home or a long Browse grid dumps the user at the top, losing their place — the costliest spot is mid-grid browsing. Gate it with useNavigationType() !== 'POP' (react-router v7) so pushes scroll to top but back restores position.
web-ux.verifyReason: Reproduced in App.jsx:11-15: `useEffect(() => window.scrollTo(0, 0), [pathname])` with no navigation-type gate, so POP navigations also scroll to top, defeating any native restoration attempt (history.scrollRestoration is never set, no <ScrollRestoration>). package.json pins react-router-dom ^7.17.0, where useNavigationType is available, so the proposed gate is implementable as stated. Back from FilmDetail to a 4-rail Home or the 21-card Browse grid lands at the top. Minor confirmed.

web-ux.title: document.title is static on every route
web-ux.detail: Title is always 'NUX — Cinema for Curious Minds'; no component ever sets document.title. Film pages, Browse and My List are indistinguishable in tabs, history and bookmarks — for a content catalog the film title in the tab is table stakes. A 5-line useEffect in FilmDetail (and per-page titles) fixes it.
web-ux.evidence: <title>NUX — Cinema for Curious Minds</title> — only occurrence; grep for document.title in src returns nothing
web-ux.verifyReason: Reproduced: grep over frontend/src for `document.title` and `Helmet` returns zero hits; the only title is index.html:6 'NUX — Cinema for Curious Minds', and the live HTML served at https://nux.ontwrpn.com/ (and every SPA route, e.g. /film/the-wickerman, /filmz — all 200 with the same 417-byte shell) carries that single static title. All routes are indistinguishable in tabs/history. Minor confirmed for a content catalog.

web-ux.title: EXTRAS (Neon Drift game, Art of Editing course) is dead data while the Games/Courses chips show 'coming soon'
web-ux.detail: EXTRAS is exported but imported nowhere (grep confirms catalog.js is the only hit), and Browse hard-empties the list for those chips: if (chip === 'Games' || chip === 'Courses') list = []. So the catalog ships a finished game record (title, meta, synopsis, generated poster in public/assets/genres) and a course record, yet the chip that should surface them says 'This shelf is coming soon'. Either wire EXTRAS into those chips (one card each beats an empty state) or delete the dead export and its two unused poster assets.
web-ux.evidence: if (chip === 'Games' || chip === 'Courses') list = []; — while EXTRAS.game/EXTRAS.course exist unused
web-ux.verifyReason: Facts reproduced: grep confirms EXTRAS appears only at its definition (catalog.js:129) — no imports anywhere — and Browse.jsx:23 hard-empties Games/Courses; the assets genre-poster-neondrift.jpg, genre-poster-course.jpg AND genre-welcome-bg.jpg (a third unused asset the finding missed) ship in frontend/public/assets/genres unused. But the user-facing half of this finding — the empty Games/Courses shelves — is explicitly on the known-intentional list ('Games/Courses chips intentionally show an empty state', flag as nit at most). What survives adversarial review is purely dead-code housekeeping: one unused export and three unused images. That is nit, not minor.

web-ux.title: Genre cards are inert buttons — a new dead-end not on the known-stub list; 5 of 12 genres have zero titles
web-ux.detail: Each genre card renders as <button type="button"> with no onClick — it affords tapping (hover styles, button semantics) and does nothing. This is not in the known-intentional list (which covers See all/footer/Play/avatar/chips). Cheapest honest fix: onClick={() => setQuery(g.label)} — the search filter already matches genre. Note also the grid advertises Thriller, History, Animation, Art House and Musical, none of which has a single catalog title (catalog genres are Film-Noir/Romance/Drama/Horror/Epic/Comedy/Crime/Sci-Fi/Documentary), so once wired those five land on the empty state; conversely Film-Noir and Epic exist but get no card.
web-ux.evidence: <button type="button" className="genre-card" key={g.id}> — no onClick handler
web-ux.verifyReason: Reproduced: Browse.jsx:79 renders each genre as `<button type="button" className="genre-card">` with no onClick — 12 focusable, hover-styled buttons that do nothing. The enumerated intentional list (See all/footer/Play/avatar/chips empty state/My List) does not include genre cards, so it cannot be refuted as known scope. Taxonomy mismatch verified against catalog.js: present genres are Film-Noir, Romance, Drama, Horror, Epic, Comedy, Crime, Sci-Fi, Documentary; the GENRES grid advertises Thriller, History, Animation, Art House, Musical (0 titles each — exactly 5 of 12), while Film-Noir and Epic get no card. The proposed setQuery(g.label) fix works because the search already matches f.genre. Minor confirmed (12-button dead-end grid plus content mismatch is more than a nit-level stub).

web-ux.title: Continue Watching progress bar assumes every film is 120 minutes
web-ux.detail: progress = Math.max(8, 100 - Math.round((item.minutesLeft / 120) * 100)). Lawrence of Arabia is 3h 47m (227 min); with 96 minutes left the user is ~58% through, but the bar shows 20%. The honesty rule applied to imagery should apply to progress too — derive from the film's runtime (parse it or store minutes in catalog) instead of a hardcoded 120.
web-ux.verifyReason: Reproduced at Rail.jsx:38 (`100 - Math.round((item.minutesLeft / 120) * 100)`, also present in the deployed bundle as `minutesLeft/120`). Math verified: Lawrence of Arabia, runtime '3h 47m' = 227 min, minutesLeft 96 → bar shows max(8, 100-80) = 20% while true progress is 131/227 ≈ 58%. The app itself displays '3h 47m' on both the Home hero meta and the FD page while the bar reads 20% — an internal contradiction, not just fake demo data. Only Lawrence has a catalog runtime to contradict the constant (the other three CW titles carry no runtime), which keeps this at minor rather than anything higher. Minor confirmed.

web-ux.title: Poster→hero morph silently skips when navigating FilmDetail → FilmDetail via More Like This (duplicate view-transition-name)
web-ux.detail: markHeroArt clears only elements tagged data-vt, but the FD hero img gets viewTransitionName:'hero-art' via React inline style with no data-vt attribute. Clicking a More-Like-This card therefore leaves TWO elements named hero-art in the outgoing snapshot (the page hero + the clicked poster) — exactly the duplicate-name condition the code's own comment warns 'skips the whole transition'. Result: the signature morph works from Home/Browse but never between film pages. Fix: also clear/tag the FD hero (give it data-vt, or query [style*="view-transition-name"]), or set the hero name via the same helper.
web-ux.verifyReason: Reproduced by code analysis: FilmDetail.jsx:26 sets viewTransitionName:'hero-art' via React inline style with NO data-vt attribute; markHeroArt (Rail.jsx:9) clears only `document.querySelectorAll('[data-vt="hero-art"]')` before tagging the clicked poster. On an FD→FD click the old-state capture therefore contains two elements named hero-art (the FD hero img, untouched by the cleanup, plus the freshly tagged poster) — the exact duplicate-name condition under which the View Transitions spec skips the entire transition (navigation still occurs, animation silently dropped), as the code's own comment at Rail.jsx:6-7 acknowledges. The deployed bundle uses the identical `[data-vt="hero-art"]` selector. Home/Browse → FD paths have only one named element and work. Minor confirmed for a silently failing polish feature on one navigation path.

web-ux.title: Catch-all route renders Home under any unknown URL — no 404, no redirect, no active nav state
web-ux.detail: <Route path="*" element={<Home />} /> shows the full Home page at e.g. /filmz or /browse/x while the wrong URL stays in the bar; combined with the FilmDetail fallback (separate finding) the app never admits a bad link. NavLinks also show no active tab on such URLs. Use <Navigate to="/" replace /> at minimum, ideally a small not-found state.
web-ux.verifyReason: Reproduced: App.jsx:27 `<Route path="*" element={<Home />} />`, present in the deployed bundle as `path:"*",element:...`; live check `curl https://nux.ontwrpn.com/filmz` returns 200 with the SPA shell, so the client renders the full Home page under /filmz with the wrong URL retained and no NavLink matching (no active tab). Unknown→Home is a common SPA convention, which keeps this at minor rather than major, but the URL is never corrected and no not-found state exists. Minor confirmed.
